speaker: interviewer
text: December 7, 1995, we're speaking with Dr. Emil Arnold. I'm Leslie Fass in Chappaqua, New York, USA. Language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wait a second. Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Today's December 7, 1995. We're speaking with Dr. Emil Arnold. I'm Leslie [? Berman ?] Fass in Chappaqua, New York, USA. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Dr. Arnold, could you give us your full name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Emil Arnold. I was born on May 20, 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Luck, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Luck for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish spelling is L-U-C-K. And it's pronounced Luck. So the phonetic spelling would be L-U-T-S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you give us your father's name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jonas. He was a physician. He practiced in Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's name was Lucy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lucy Schecter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- still have one sister, Eugenie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, um, what was her married name? What is her married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her married name, uh, was Duke. But, uh, she is divorced at the moment. And her name is, uh, still Arnold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other relatives living with you when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, yes. Grandparents visited us once, uh, in a while. And my cousin Gizella visited us just before the war. She stayed with us for about a year. And then, uh, when the war started, she, uh, continued to live with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these your paternal grandparents or your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are mother's, uh, parents. I was more familiar with my mother's family, uh, than with my father's family. I did meet my paternal grandparents. Uh-- but we had more of an occasion to, uh, visit with my father's-- my mother's family. They owned an estate. And we spent summers, uh, with them. They didn't really own the estate. Uh-- Jews were not allowed to own any property in Poland. Uh-- property belonged to Polish nobility. This happened to be a prince who owned a very large parcel of land. And he would lease it to our grandparents. And they managed the estate. And, uh, we visited them, um, spent summers with them in the country usually. So this is where we, uh, got together with, uh, with the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Markus. Grandmother was Rachel Schecter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have cousins that would congregate there with you at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We, uh-- my mother had, uh, three sisters. Uh-- one sister emigrated to the US, uh, before I was born. And, uh, the other two sisters died during the Holocaust. Hanna was, uh, one sister. And Malvina was the other sister. Hanna had two daughters, Gisella, whom I mentioned, and her brother, who also died in the Holocaust. Malvina had, uh, two daughters, Bella and Suzan. And they and their father, Karol, survived the Holocaust. Their mother, Malvina, also died. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this estate or this parcel of land that they rented?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- well, there was actually more than one. Uh-- one was called [POLISH] another one's [POLISH]. And that was in the Polish province of Galicia, about, oh, I don't know, 100 miles or so away from where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you get there from Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-- sometimes we drove. We had two cars and a chauffeur. And, uh, we would drive. Most of the time, we went by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this would be during the summertime?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was during summer vacations and yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of your father's interests in addition to being a physician?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a physician. He was very dedicated to his, uh, practice. He had many patients. And, uh, that's really what I remember most. He had very few, uh, uh, side occupations or hobbies. I know he played chess. And so he taught me how to play chess. We did that. I collected stamps. And he helped me with this. He was not a stamp collector himself. But my mother and my father were-- liked music. But they didn't play, uh, instruments. But they insisted that I-- that I study music. I studied violin and later switched to piano.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe family holidays, perhaps a Passover Seder and who was at the table, how it looked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-- My parents were not very religious. They did observe all the holidays. We had the traditional Passover Seder. On Saturdays, my mother would light candles. I remember her, uh, covering, uh, her face or her eyes with her hands. And I wasn't sure whether she was crying or saying a prayer. On Passover, we had a regular, uh, service, uh, perhaps not very strict. But we went through the Haggadah. The fun part was when I got to search for the afikomen matzoh. So that-- that's what I remember, not very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you search for it by yourself or with your cousins or your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember my sister. I guess she was very young. [INAUDIBLE] my sister's four years younger than-- than myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your cousins at the table, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. I-- I imagine that when my cousin Gizella visited us she must have been. Many memories are sort of sketchy. Certain things I do remember. Other things I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother preparing for the Passover Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We did have a cook who did most of the cooking. Mother supervised mostly. We had, uh, two maids. One was a cook. And, uh, as I understand, if I recall, she was a very good cook, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mila was the cook. We also had another maid, Vera. She-- she was a maid and a nurse. She really took care of-- of the kids most of the time. So we spent a lot of time with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your home, if was a house or an apartment, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in an apartment house. Uh-- we occupied, as I remember, the entire second floor of the house. My father had his office in the apartment. Uh-- they-- there was a two-room office and a waiting room also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was one part of the house taken up with-- with his office, his medical equipment, and so on. And the other part of the apartment, uh, was the living room, several bedrooms, maid's room, and a kitchen. So it was a very, uh, comfortable large house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, I remember that we had electricity. We had running water. We had a toilet and a bathtub with running water. Fire had to be made with wood and coal. And that's how the house was heated, with coal in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who got the wood and coal?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was brought in by horse and buggy, I guess. There was a pile of it outside. And the maid would bring it in and start a fire, keep it all night. In the morning she has to light the fire to make breakfast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the maid lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they both did.

speaker: interviewer
text: The maid--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a maid's room, which was very small. So I remember there was one bed in the maid's room where one maid slept. I'm not sure where the other maid sl-- slept. Perhaps she slept in the kitchen or maybe in the same room. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a Jewish section of Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was not a Jewish section. It was a residential street. Uh-- across the house, I remember, was a post office. It was a very large building. Uh-- along the street, there were least two other physicians who lived on the same street. And I-- I do remember they visited us often socially. So most of our family friends were other physicians. And as far as I remember, they were all Jewish, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: These other physicians were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I remember, the, uh-- most of them were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to public school or Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a public school. Uh-- I think it was called public school number eight. It was a Polish school. Uh-- most of the children there were Catholic. There were few Jews I remember, so. But in those days, none of the children were really traditional orthodox Jews. Just by looking at them, you couldn't tell really that they were Jewish. And I experienced very little, in those days, very little feeling of anti-Semitism. And I had friends who were Jewish and non-Jewish equally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you ever experience anti-Semitism within the school or outside of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bef-- before-- before the war, I don't have any recollection of it at all. I remember conversations about it. There were restrictions, uh, against Jews. There were certain things Jews were not allowed to do. My father, for example, finished medical school in Czechoslovakia in Prague, because Jews were not allowed, uh, to attend medical school in Poland. Once he had his medical degree, he could, uh, practice in Poland, passed the uh, license examination. But he ad to go abroad to get his degree. But personally, I don't remember, uh, any incidents of anti-Semitism before the war. I do remember a number during and after the war. But before the war, I'm sure that it happened. But I-- I can't think of anything specific.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents ever discussing anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not specifically, yes. But it was a common topic on-- of conversation. Apparently, shortly before the war, perhaps a year before the war, the, uh, laws against the Jews were becoming more common Uh-- they were trying to restrict production of kosher meat, I remember, and things like that. Perhaps what was happening in Germany at that time had some influence over what the, uh, Poles were thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other restrictions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any specific-- any specific restrictions or personal, uh, incidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I, I-- I had a number, perhaps an equal number, of non-Jewish friends and Jewish friends. Uh-- most of the neighboring kids in the immediate neighborhood, I guess, were Jewish. But not-- not all of them, because I do remember non-- non-Jewish friends. In fact, I would say that my best friend at that time in first and second grade was-- was a Catholic boy. Uh-- the schools were, uh, non-- non-sectarian, non-religious. But at the beginning of the day, uh, everybody said a prayer. And, uh, I was instructed specifically that when we were saying a prayer not to cross myself. I'm just supposed to stand and not say anything. They told me to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any religious instruction that you were receiving at that time inside or outside of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in school, no. I had Hebrew lessons privately. I had a Hebrew teacher for several years. And I studied Hebrew, both Hebrew language and the Bible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting at what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. I would guess age four or five, perhaps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she, uh-- she kept the house. She brought up her children. She helped my father. Uh-- he didn't have a, a nurse. So once in a while, she would assist him with-- in his practice. He also was, I remember, a believer in, uh, natural medicine. So he had a number of natural, uh, preparations, herbs and things like that. My mother would help mixing these things, preparing them. Other than that, uh, she had an active social life. She read a lot. And they had many friends. They visited friends and friends came to visit in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her friends were primarily Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, those that I can think of, the closest friends, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be some of your fondest or one of your fondest memories from that time period before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't single anything out specifically. I had a fairly well-regulated life. Like usually, one remembers unpleasant things, more-- more than pleasant things. I hated studying violin, for example, even though I liked music. I had a very bad, uh, violin teacher. Every time he didn't like the way I played, when I hit a wrong note, he would knock the bow down on the floor. I had to bend down and pick it up and start all over again. It would happen again and again. I can't understand why I didn't complain. Finally, I switched to the piano. I liked vacations. When we-- we went to the country, it was a big change of pace. I-- I enjoyed that very much. I liked driving in the car. We had two cars. One of them was a convertible. Driving in the country in a convertible was a thrilling experience. I liked climbing trees. I remember that. We had a big acacia tree in the backyard. I climbed that. So snatches of memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have a garden behind your house? What was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know whether you call it a garden. I-- I suppose you could. I don't remember any grass there. But there were trees and, uh, and some plants.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, exclusively. My parents spoke Yiddish. They never taught me Yiddish. Uh-- they spoke German and French also. But, uh, among ourselves, the, the only language was, uh, was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did you and your sister have separate rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your awareness of the forthcoming war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, around 1938, '39 I was, what, six years old, 6 and 1/2. Uh-- There were increasing rumors that, uh, something was coming. Of course everybody was hoping that, that things would resolve themselves. Uh-- But. I think [INAUDIBLE] suddenly, I remember what, uh-- what happened was that one day we saw German planes overhead. Everybody came out to look. Then we saw bombs flying down. And, uh, there wasn't very much damage. But there were several houses, two houses I can-- can recall with the front wall missing. I don't remember anybody getting hurt in this. So there was some property damage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very scary time. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was 1939 when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: In September?

speaker: interviewee
text: September, I guess, yes. And, uh, well, it was a very scary time. For me, as a kid, I thought it was sort of exciting to see these planes flying overhead and dropping bombs. Uh-- well, when they started bombing, we went to a shelter in the basement. There was large bomb shelter. So we hid there. And everybody was prepared for Germans to, uh, to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the shelter look like? Was it part of your house or something that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: --especially prepared?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a different house, perhaps two or three houses away. And I don't really remember what-- why there was such a big basement in that particular house. But it was underground. And, uh, it was rather large, the number of people who would go there to, uh-- to hide from the bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Polish people, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The entire neighborhood, yeah. Well, that particular period in 1939 didn't last very long. Everybody expected Germans to come in. Fortunately, we had a reprieve, because Hitler and Stalin had an accord. And, uh, it was the Russians who occupied that part of Poland, the Ukraine. so, uh,

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe where Luck was located in Poland, where it's situated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck is-- Luck is-- Luck is located, oh, perhaps 60, 80 miles west from the then Russian border. The next town to the east Rowne. And the border was not far from the Russian border. So when Russians came, they didn't have very far to go to occupy Luck. So that part of the country, the Ukraine, was under the Russian occupation between 1939 and 1941 when the war started between Germany and Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if your parents made any preparation or any attempts to leave Poland prior to 1939 or-- or at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I remember, uh, there were conversations. My mother's sister, Regina, uh, went to, uh, America. We had family there. My-- my, uh-- I guess it was grandfather's family. Uh-- part of his family moved to the US.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my Aunt Regina went to visit them. I believe that they lived in Philadelphia. And she decided to stay there. So, uh, my parents were talking about, uh, perhaps going there for a visit during the World Fair in New York, just use this is an excuse to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, I remember that there were perhaps not plans, but at least discussions about doing this. But somehow it never materialized. So we just stayed there. Uh, When, uh, the war started, many people fled the Western part of Poland from Warsaw. And I remember there was a, uh, whole caravans of cars, people from Polish government and, uh, other people who could afford it. They were passing through--

speaker: interviewer
text: Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: --through Luck. And some of them stayed temporarily in our house, somehow j-- just to rest. There were several Jewish physicians who also fled from, uh, from the western part of Poland. Some of them became very good friends. And, uh, I remember a very good friend of mine who was among them. Uh, his father was a physician. And they came from western part of Poland. My piano teacher was a concert pianist from, uh, from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she was-- her name was Branislava. I think her last name was Rosenbaum if I remember. And, uh-- later on, when we had to, uh-- several years later, we were in ghetto, she actually lived with us during that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this Aunt Regina who went to Philadelphia, was that Regina Schecter, your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, her married name was Morrison.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she was your mother's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's sister. Her maiden name was Schecter, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice any other changes in your life? You were very young. But any other changes in your life at that time, in the late '30s? You said there was a-- a caravan of people coming from Warsaw to Luck. How were they received by the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a lot of sarcasm, uh, uh, that-- that, uh-- the high Polish officials, including army officials, instead of fighting the Germans, they, uh-- they fled. Eventually, many of them wound up in England. So there was a big Polish colony, uh, in England, uh, during the war and after the war who escaped. But I guess it's a natural thing to do, to, uh, to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other Jewish families in Luck, did they receive these people warmly? Were these people from Warsaw, did any of them stay in Luck? Or did they all pass through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this physician, for example, I believe his last name was Frohm. I remember he was an obstetrician. He and his family did stay in Luck. So I-- I think a number of people, especially the Jews, uh, did stay, uh, in Luck. So there was a, uh, certain number of refugees from, uh, German-occupied Polish territories who did stay in Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they weren't resented by the local population, the local Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of any incidents. I would say definitely, definitely no. No, I don't see any reason why they would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what type of, um, literature was in your home growing up, what type of newspapers, Yiddish newspapers, Polish newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish newspapers I don't remember. In my-- my father's office, there was a large library. I remember the collection of medical books. My mother read a lot. She, she, uh, read classics and, uh-- uh, so we had a lot of books of that sort in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in, in sports growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not organized sports that I remember. I, uh-- I did like to climb trees and run and swim. I liked swimming. We had a river called River Styr in the, uh-- in Luck. And in summer, we would go and swim in the river both as a family, and also I went with-- with my friends to swim in the river. So that was a big, uh, favorite recreation. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you want to hear what happened later when the Russians came, uh, things changed. Uh-- [PAUSE] The, uh, Russians called themselves the liberators. There were slogans all over that now we liberated, uh, the Ukrainian and eastern part of Poland. I didn't quite understand what they mean by liberating. I always felt we were fairly, uh, liberated. But, uh-- How did your parents feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, uh-- my father happened to get a job as a physician for the, what was called, NKVD, Russian secret police, which later became KGB. I don't know how he got the job. He was certainly not a communist. In fact, he hated communism. Was very openly critical of them. So somehow, I don't know through connections or, or what, even though he was very outspoken against communists and Russians in general, he-- he did get the job. I remember he didn't speak Russian very well either. But-- so that was a good thing economically. Because you have access to special stores where we can buy food and other things that were not available to other people. The school where I went became a Russian language school, too. Many Russian officials came to, uh-- to, uh, to Poland, uh, with the occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your teachers change?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers did change. We did not have any Russian teachers. I remember that the new teacher was actually a Jewish woman who lived in Luck. He spoke-- she spoke fluent Russian somehow. And, uh-- so her, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.
